phenomenon: cognitive-flexibility in dual-demand switching
claim: routine code-switching practice enhances capacity for stimulus-response reconfiguration independent of verbal domain

phenomenon: retrospective inference about sequence-length from outcome rarity
claim: rare outcomes are mentally attributed to longer preceding sequences regardless of actual causal probability

phenomenon: decision-choice justification under regret and accountability awareness
claim: internal regret consciousness triggers critical self-review eliminating justification-acceptance bias, whereas external accountability maintains it

phenomenon: belief-persistence after discredited causal explanation
claim: providing alternate causal explanation displaces disconfirmed information more effectively than denial alone

phenomenon: performance suppression under stereotype-activated group consciousness
claim: gender-stereotype activation suppresses domain-performance through situational pressure rather than stable capability

phenomenon: semantic-warmth to thermal-perception transfer
claim: thinking about social-connection concepts modulates physical temperature experience

phenomenon: distinct behavioral-emotional phenotypes within personality-pathology syndrome
claim: comorbid psychopathy amplifies emotion-dysfunction and criminal-severity within antisocial syndrome, distinguishing two subtypes

phenomenon: disengagement from failing commitments and escalating investment
claim: three distinct interventions reduce entrapment through threat-reduction, minimum-thresholds, and process-focused accountability

phenomenon: personality-trait prediction of leadership-behavior style
claim: extraversion and agreeableness predict charismatic-influence independent of outcome-management orientation

phenomenon: time-dependent reversal in regret type (action vs. inaction)
claim: actions produce acute regret but inactions compound into enduring regret through expanding could-have-been possibilities

phenomenon: unconscious defensive response amplification from mortality-awareness
claim: death-related thoughts amplify group-favoritism only when suppressed outside awareness, not when consciously contemplated

phenomenon: long-term formation of relational orientations (prosocial vs. competitive)
claim: prosocial orientation roots in secure attachment and larger families, intensifies across adult lifespan

phenomenon: regulatory-capacity depletion from prior effortful self-control
claim: effortful self-control impairs subsequent regulatory-capacity as though drawing from finite metabolic-neural resource

phenomenon: culture-specific preference for individuality vs. conformity
claim: cultural norms shape and reinforce individual preferences for conformity vs. differentiation through self-selection feedback

phenomenon: distinct psychological well-being trajectories in midlife-adulthood
claim: two independent well-being dimensions generate three distinct mature profiles, mediated by identity-formation processes

phenomenon: differential emotional response to public exposure and self-evaluation
constructs: shame guilt public-exposure self-appraisal moral-transgression conscience
claim: shame emerges from public-exposure combined with negative-self-evaluation; guilt from private-conscience and remorse

phenomenon: stereotype-congruence blocking self-domain identification
claim: gender-domain stereotype incompatibility reduces self-identification with domain even among individuals skilled in it

phenomenon: developmental continuity of personality and conduct across decade
claim: childhood personality and social-competence predict adult personality and relationship-success through continuity

phenomenon: cross-cultural variation in attribution from behaviorally-constrained action
claim: Americans show correspondence-bias regardless of behavioral-diagnosticity; Japanese suppress it when behavior is situationally-forced

phenomenon: personality-to-behavior prediction consistency across measurement instruments
claim: Big Five personality factors reliably predict complex socially-significant behaviors across multiple assessment approaches
